text: The video shows a step-by-step laparoscopic heart man's reversal. The patient is a fifty-one year old man, who underwent emergency laparotomy for perforated diverticular disease. Sigmoid resection and colostomy, with resection of distal jejunum, were performed on July twenty nineteen. After seven months, reconstruction of GI continuity was planned.

The video shows the placement of surgical ports on a patient in the modified Lloyd Davies position. A Hassan trocar is used to enter the abdomen, followed by the placement of a 5mm trocar in the right flank and a 12mm port in the right iliac fossa. After stoma closure, a gel point is used to place an additional trocar off the stoma site to aid in mobilizing the splenic flexure. The surgeon begins access and adhesiolysis using bipolar energy to divide adhesions.

The surgeon carefully dissects small bowel adhesions and identifies the rectal stump using non-absorbable stitches from a previous operation. Diathermy is used to free the rectal stump, while an assistant introduces a probe transanally to aid in stump identification and provide counter traction. The rectal stump is highlighted, and intramesorectal dissection is performed to minimize nerve damage, despite potential increased bleeding. The dissection is complete when the rectum is sufficiently mobilized for safe anastomosis. The colostomy is closed at this stage.

Indocyanine green (ICG) is applied to assess bowel perfusion and determine the optimal site for colon transection. The colon is marked with ICG, and diluted iodine povidone solution is used to detect any inadvertent bowel injury. The colon is then opened, and a purse string suture is placed.

The surgeon places the anvil and introduces the bowel into the abdomen. A gel point is positioned at the stoma site, and an additional trocar is inserted to facilitate the procedure. The splenic flexure is mobilized to reduce tension, and the inferior mesenteric vein is divided. An end-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed, with perfusion checked using indocyanine green, confirming adequate serration of both ends.
An intraoperative endoscopy is performed, showing the final appearance of the surgical site. A drain is placed in the pelvis, and the test results are negative.
